body:
Hi John,

We have identified the issue. There was a minor syntax error in how those specific permissions need to be referenced within the Mercury language.

You need to use "api_read" and "data_write" instead of colons. We are updating our documentation to be more explicit about this.

Please let us know if you have any further questions.

Regards,
Aetheros Support

subject: Re: server is rejecting mail from single domain
body: James D. Parra wrote: > James D. Parra wrote: > >> Hello, >> >> We have a client who just this month is getting the following error when >> sending mail to us; >> >> The following recipient(s) could not be reached: >> >> oyopvgi@ourdomain.com on 2/8/2008 4:17 PM >> There was a SMTP communication problem with the recipient's >> email server. Please contact your system administrator. >> >> : Helo command >> rejected: need fully-qualified hostname> >> >> >> >> My postfix log says the following; >> >> Feb 8 12:21:04 postserver postfix/smtpd[22189]: warning: 64.22.240.195: >> address not listed for hostname ns.ky3.com >> Feb 8 12:21:05 postserver postfix/smtpd[22189]: NOQUEUE: reject: RCPT >> > from > >> unknown[64.22.240.195]: 504 : Helo command rejected: need >> fully-qualified hostname; from= >> > to= > >> proto=ESMTP helo= >> >> >> What can I add to the main.cf to allow this IP address or domain access? >> >> > > Tell the client to fix his helo name. "Spam-Server" is more than non > fqdn. it's a provocation! > > if you prefer to surrender, then remove reject_non_fqdn_hostname (or > reject_non_fqdn_helo_hostname). > ~~~~~ > > I can suggest that to him, but this is a client so I can only ask. From the > client's vantage point, our server is the only one rejecting his mail, so I > need to tread politely. > Of course you need to find the right words... Be helpful and firm. Tell them: - you will whitelist them (so you're very open) - but you don't want anyone else to be whitelisted. so they must own the IP - they must take responsibility for all mail coming out of that IP - they may have delivery issues elsewhere. they may not realize it, but some sites may be discarding their mail. And if that didn't happen yet, chances are this will happen in the future. Many people are getting sick of supporting zombies because of broken sites. In short, their system is not configured according to the standards: - a helo name must be an fqdn hostname (or an IP literal) - their system must have a correct reverse DNS: IP->name->IP must result in the original IP. > Concerning removing reject_non_fqdn_hostname (or > reject_non_fqdn_helo_hostname) is not something I want to do. If this > weren't a client of ours I wouldn't worry too much about it, but it is and I > must. Is there a way could add an exception to the main.cf for this domain > or IP address? > It would have been easier if you showed your config. you need to put a check_client_access that returns OK but you must not put this in the wrong place. here is an acceptable example: smtpd_recipient_restrictions = ... reject_unauth_destination reject_non_fqdn_sender reject_non_fqdn_recipient ... reject_invalid_hostname check_client_access cidr:/etc/postfix/broken_client reject_non_fqdn_hostname ... == broken_client 10.1.2.3/32 OK In short, you should move your reject_non_fqdn_hostname down enough to be able to add the broken_client check before it without affecting other checks. a less appealing alternative is to make your reject_non_fqdn_hostname a result of a check_client_access: == 10.1.2.3/32 DUNNO 0.0.0.0/0 reject_non_fqdn_hostname but this will only add complexity.

subject: TKO NOTICE: eBay Registration Suspension - Possible Unauthorized Account !
body: Dear eBay member Dear eBay member , Due to recent activity, including possible unauthorized listings, we have temporarily suspended activity on your account in order to allow us to investigate this matter further. If you believe that this action may have been taken in error, or, if you feel that your account may have been tampered with, please Sign In and contact our Live Help team so that we can provide additional information and work with you to resolve this issue. In the event that you are unable to contact eBay through Live Help after taking these steps, respond directly to this message to request assistance. We will contact you by email after we have received your response. Please allow at least 72 hours for an email reply. Emailing us prior to receiving our reply will result in an additional delay. In order to handle your concern as quickly and efficiently as possible, we encourage you to contact us through Live Help if you are able to do so. If you received this notice and you are not the authorized account holder, please be aware that it is in violation of eBay policy to represent oneself as another eBay user. Such action may also be in violation of local, national, and/or international law. eBay is committed to assist law enforcement with any inquiries related to attempts to misappropriate personal information with the intent to commit fraud or theft. Information will be provided at the request of law enforcement agencies to ensure that perpetrators are prosecuted to the fullest extent of the law. Regards, Safeharbor Department eBay Inc.

subject: Conference on Email and Anti-Spam August 2-3
body: FINAL CALL FOR PARTICIPATION CEAS 2007 -- Fourth Conference on Email and Anti-Spam Aug 2 and 3, 2007 (Thursday, Friday) Mountain View, California http://www.ceas.cc The organizers of the Conference on Email and Anti-Spam (CEAS 2007) invite you to participate in its fourth annual event. CEAS has established itself as the major venue for Email related research from both academia and industry, with topic covering all aspects of electronic messaging including email, instant messaging, text messaging, and VoIP. The conference will be held as usual in Mountain View, California from Aug 2 to Aug 3, 2007. This forum brings together academic and industrial researchers to present new work in all aspects of email and messaging, including uses and abuses such as spam. A special event associated with CEAS 2007 is the Live Spam Challenge. (http://www.ceas.cc/2007/challenge/challenge.html) We encourage you to check the CEAS web site for the registration and advance program as well as up-to-date conference information, including * Conference schedule * Live Spam Challenge * Accepted papers * Conference facility * Hotel information Links of Registration and Hotel reservation are also provided in this message: * Registration: Please check the registration online at https://www.acteva.com/booking.cfm?bevaid=133635 * Hotel reservation: http://www.ceas.cc/2007/hotel2007 For other questions related to CEAS 2007, please contact us via (received by all the chairs) --++**==--++**==--++**==--++**==--++**==--++**==--++**== ceas-announce mailing list hwgf-qazypeen@lists.stanford.edu https://mailman.stanford.edu/mailman/listinfo/ceas-announce

subject: Can the federal government restore public faith?
body: First Up Plus, Canadian households in huge debt and a rise in pregnant people using cannabis ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ First Up By Manuela Vega Good morning. Here’s the latest on reports of foreign interference in elections, Canadian household debt and cannabis use during pregnancy. DON’T MISS Canadian Press/Sean Kilpatrick federal politics David Johnston has rejected a public inquiry and accused Canada’s spies of mismanaging foreign interference information Although a public inquiry into foreign elections meddling would have been an “easy” option, it wouldn’t lead to a full understanding of Chinese foreign interference since classified intelligence must stay secret, special rapporteur David Johnston says in a report. An inquiry would actually delay the action needed to close the gaps and duplicate what’s already been done. It also wouldn’t allow for a judicial commission to publicly share more than he could. Stephanie Levitz and Tonda MacCharles report on why Johnston will, instead, hold public hearings to produce another report in October that he says is needed to restore public trust. More: Johnston said media leaks on foreign interference were incomplete. Here’s what he found. Meanwhile: Pierre Poilievre is calling on Johnston to resign and is refusing to review classified information on foreign state interference. Susan Delacourt’s take: The much-awaited report on foreign meddling in Canadian elections rests on a bold idea: that the institutions of our democracy are capable of restoring faith in the system. Has Johnston seen politics lately? Canadian Press File Photo economy Canadian households now have the worst debt ratio of any G7 country As household debt decreased in the U.S., U.K. and Germany, and remained nearly unchanged in Italy, it rose “inexorably” in Canada, according to a report by the Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation. Behind about three-quarters of that debt? Mortgages and high interest rates straining household finances, the report’s author writes. Jeremy Nuttall reports on why debt levels will continue to grow unless housing unaffordability is addressed — and how young families are the most affected. By the numbers: “At the time of the recession in 2008, (household debt) stood at about 80 per cent of the size of the economy, in 2010 it rose to 95 per cent, and by 2021 debt exceeded its size,” the report reads. It adds that at the end of 2021, Canada’s household debt-to-GDP ratio reached 107 per cent. Interactive: Use our tool to see how home prices have changed in your Toronto neighbourhood over the last decade. Canadian Press/Tijana Martin health The number of pregnant Ontarians requiring hospital treatment for cannabis use has almost doubled since 2018 A new study has found the legalization of cannabis has been associated with an increase over time in hospital visits for cannabis use during pregnancy, Megan Ogilvie writes. The authors of the report, published in the Canadian Medical Association Journal, say their conclusion suggests more targeted public health messaging is needed. Here’s what we know about the increase in the “rare but severe events” — and the associated risks. By the numbers: The absolute increase in those needing hospital care over the study period was small, the researchers stress, rising from 11 per 100,000 pregnancies to 20 per 100,000 pregnancies. Why it matters: Babies of people who received hospital care for cannabis use were more likely to be born preterm and require admission to the neonatal intensive care unit, the study found. More: Although the Society of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists of Canada recommends people avoid cannabis during pregnancy, its use during pregnancy has become more common. One 2019 U.S. study found about seven per cent of pregnant women used cannabis in 2017 — up from 3.4 per cent in 2003. Need a trusty guide through the housing crunch? Living Here, the Star’s free newsletter, can help. Each week, it’ll bring you the analysis and expert advice you need to navigate renting, buying, owning or just getting by in and around Toronto — plus more of the Star’s essential real estate coverage. Sign up for free here. WHAT ELSE “Bring it on.” Here’s how Doug Ford reacted to Bonnie Crombie’s exploratory bid for Liberal leader. Parents were “blindsided” by Peel school board’s decision to strip the name of a Black activist from new a centre. These mayoral candidates’ photos with uniformed Toronto cops has been slammed as “improper.” Which candidates for mayor are actually offering real talk on Toronto taxes? Too few this far into the race. The Peel school board is investigating after an anonymous letter alleged “violence, fear” at a Mississauga middle school. An Ontario jail guard is guilty of sexually assaulting a prisoner. Here’s why his account of events was “completely unbelievable.” Was the “incel” killer a terrorist? A court hears landmark arguments in the Toronto spa murder. Is AI coming for your job? These are the workers who will be replaced first, according to experts. Verified travellers will be able to pass through security faster at major Canadian airports — bypassing these screening steps as they go through checkpoints. Actors have spoken out about their distaste for it — has the onscreen sex scene gasped its last climactic breath? Toronto is getting a Pink Floyd exhibit, “an audio-visual sensorial journey unlike any other.” This bike locking ring with a bad lean has pedestrians bent out of shape. ICYMI Ella Olsson/Unsplash Families are having meals catered instead of buying groceries. We crunched the numbers and spoke with experts to find out if it’s really cheaper. CLOSE-UP Steven Shpak Photography DON RIVER: A mink, named “Eddie” by Toronto photographer Steven Shpak, emerges “from a snow-covered log, making his way over to his frozen pond somewhere in the Don Valley." Eddie’s picture is just one of many bringing joy to thousands of people online. Take a look at Shpak’s charming photos of fox, eagles, deer and owls. Thank you for reading. You can reach me and the First Up team at firstup@thestar.ca. I’ll see you back here tomorrow. subject: mportant update about our crypto services 
body: We're pausing our crypto services We’ve temporarily paused our crypto services, effective immediately Hi Fiona, We regret to inform you that we've temporarily paused Revolut Crypto services until further notice in Hungary, effective immediately. Other Revolut services won't be affected by this change. Why are services being paused? We're unable to provide services right now due to recent changes in Hungarian legislation — but we're working hard to ensure that these services can be resumed as soon as possible. What services are affected? You won't be able to: Place any buy orders Stake new assets Deposits will be rejected You can still use the app to: Sell your crypto Withdraw crypto to an external wallet (please note this is only available for certain tokens) What do I need to do? subject: Re: Talks by leading experts at Pain Research 2026
body:
Dear Researcher,

Greetings of the day.
I hope this message finds you well.

This email is in regards to your interest to be as a Speaker / Delegate at our upcoming 3rd World Summit on Pain Medicine & Anesthesia, organized by Times Scientific Group, is scheduled to take place on July 13-14, 2026 at Berlin, Germany.

Our conference covers a wide range of topics, including Chronic Pain, Anesthesia, Psychological Pain, Emergency Medical Care, Neuropathic Pain, Nursing in Critical Care, Neuromodulation and Interventional Pain, Regenerative Pain, Intensive care, Perioperative Care, Integrative Medicine, Musculoskeletal pain, Surgical Pain, Cancer pain, Orthopedic, Trauma, Acupuncture, Physical Medicine, Rehabilitation, and Palliative Care, and more Pain and Anesthesia related subspecialties.

On the occasion of our company anniversary, we are pleased to offer a 30% discount on the registration fee for confirmed speakers.

It is our way of expressing gratitude for the valuable insights you'll bring to the conference.

To proceed, kindly send us the title of your presentation and your abstract at your earliest convenience.

We are truly excited about the potential for collaboration and the opportunity to have you join us at Pain Research 2026.

If you have any questions or need further information, please don't hesitate to reach out.

Looking forward to hearing from you!

Regards,
Joy Flynn
Program Manager

WhatsApp: +1 707-378-6010

subject: Where TabletPC will succeed (Tech Update)
body:
Tech Update Today

VITAL SIGNS FOR JULY 17, 2002

David Berlind
Where TabletPC will succeed--and why
OK, OK, you readers were right all along. I now believe that Microsoft"s TabletPC can be a big hit. Where? Vertical markets. Why? Development tools.
Why you won't be buying one from Dell

Latest from ZDNet News

CERT: Security reports multiplying

Microsoft squashes Windows 2000 bugs

Sun's ID software gets a Liberty face-lift

Mobile slump puts the bite on Bluetooth

'Rainbow' coalition to spread wireless

Handspring courts corporate buyers

Apple patches serious security hole

More Enterprise News

Farber's Picks
Dan
Farber Slowing sales shrinking Intel workforce
Intel announced lower-than-expected earnings yesterday and that 4,000 jobs (5 percent of its staff) would be eliminated this year. This may bring Intel's margins back in line, but the overall outlook is not bright in the coming months, despite Federal Reserve Chairman Alan Greenspan's optimism about a more timely recovery.
Read the full story VMware ups consolidation ante
The new version of VMware's lower-end server product is slated to run atop the latest versions of Linux from SuSE, MandrakeSoft and Red Hat, as well as Microsoft Windows .Net Server due this year. Offering more Intel-server based consolidation opportunities than ever, GSX Server 2.0 includes support for AMD's Athlon XP and Intel's Xeon processors.
Read the full story
Server consolidation eases system management Software contracts: Clause for alarm
In the high-stakes game of corporate software negotiations, the silver-tongued sales rep isn't your biggest challenge: It's the language in the contracts. Enterprise customers spend surprisingly little time pouring over the fine print of multi-million dollar software contracts that are rife with obscure terminology, vague expansion charges, and mind-boggling license conversions. As a result, they end up paying in ways they had never imagined long after the deal is signed. Just ask California.
Read the fine print Six steps to the best TCO with Linux
Don't just bring in Linux as yet another OS to manage. To help you determine whether Linux offers better TCO than Windows, Gartner examines the benefits and pitfalls of deploying Linux-based servers.
Read the full story Rack server a la mode
With its Xserve rack-mount server, Apple is moving into enterprise territory. The specs are impressive: Dual 1GHz PowerPC G4s, up to 2GB DDR SDRAM, two 64-bit 66MHz PCI slots (plus a third combination PCI/AGP slot), dual Gigabit Ethernet, FireWire and USB ports, and four Ultra ATA/100 Apple Drive Module bays. But Bill O'Brien thinks Apple's licensing may be even more appealing than the hardware.
Read the full story A notebook with desktop muscle
WinBook's big and heavy J4 uses a desktop Pentium 4 and leaves mobile P4-M notebooks in the dust. That means heavy lifting for your spreadsheets--and your shoulders. If you travel only occasionally and need top performance, this might be the sole computer for you.
Read the full reviewWrite me at dan.farber@cnet.com
